topic: A neighbor wants to install a fence
raw_output:
[story 1]

The sound of the hammer tapping against the wooden stakes was a familiar, irritating rhythm. I watched from behind the lace curtains, my hands clasped tightly in my lap. It had been three weeks since Mr. Henderson first mentioned his plans for a new boundary. Each morning, the same scene: his truck idling too long, the faint scent of sawdust drifting over the shared hedge. I didn't bother to look up when he approached my mailbox; I just waited for him to finish his assessment, the slight tightening around my jaw the only outward sign of my internal state. It was just another thing in the landscape, another imposition on the quiet routine I had meticulously built.

[story 2]

"Just need to get this done before the winter sets in," Mr. Henderson had said, his voice booming slightly across the lawn. I nodded once, a small, jerky movement, and turned back to my book. I felt the familiar dull ache behind my eyes, the kind that comes from anticipating an unavoidable inconvenience. I traced the pattern on my worn armchair cushion, focusing intently on the weave of the fabric. The thought of having to confront him again, to offer some form of polite, non-committal response, felt like lifting a stone. It was simply what happened, the predictable progression of neighborhood changes.

[story 3]

I poured a cup of tea, the steam doing little to warm the chill that had settled deep in my chest. The afternoon sun slanted across the driveway, illuminating the freshly cut grass where Mr. Henderson was already measuring the posts. I watched his methodical movements—the way he checked the level, the precise placement of each support. There was no anger in my posture, only a slow deflation. I took a slow sip of tea, letting the silence stretch between us, a heavy, unmoving thing. It was settled now; this fence was coming, and I would simply accommodate it.
